title: The Macro Minute: What's more likely: a short squeeze, correction, sustained recovery, or crash?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, with our
macro minute for Tuesday, July 21st,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note, so let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, what's
more likely? A short squeeze, a
correction, a sustained recovery, or
crash?
The short answer is all four, in that
order.
In terms of the analytical nuance, uh
record bearish US stock bets clash with
an S&P 500 index rally up 18% since late
March. S&P 500 short interest nears 3.8%
of free float, a record in data since
2010. Russell 3000 short interest hit a
record 6.3%. Recall that the Russell
3000 is the largest um you know, it's
the largest equity index that we have in
terms of um featuring the the maximum
amount of companies. Uh the build-up of
near record short exposure has prevented
broader contagion at the index level in
the US equities asset class via the
positive influence of van and charm
flows. This dynamic is supported by
all-time high levels of dispersion among
index constituents, according to
all-time low ratings of S&P 500 3-month
implied volatility correlations, signal
by our positioning model. This implies
the broad market will be relatively
insulated until a surprising enough
macro or micro catalyst forcibly shifts
the narrative. White swans need not
apply.
The build-up of financial leverage
leverage is near the top of investor
concerns. Retail margin debt is now up
over 100% on a trailing 3-year basis,
officially confirming fears of an AI
bubble and the secular bear market that
is likely to follow it.
The only other peaks that rival the
latest readings are 109%
in January 1994, plus 123% in March of
1998, ahead of a 19% drawdown in the S&P
500, plus 173% retail margin debt in
March of 2000, ahead of an 83% drawdown
in the Nasdaq, and plus 104% retail
margin debt in July 2007, ahead of a 57%
drawdown in the S&P 500.
The bull case requires fading the base
rate of a 75% chance of a stock market
crash.
Worse, retail margin debt has breached
5.1% of total US household debt. The
only other higher peak was the 6%
recorded in March 2000 ahead of the S&P
500 getting cut in half and the NASDAQ
getting cut in half more than twice.
As always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
credit default swaps now above the
50-day moving average.
Says, "Does anyone else know that we
tend
that we do tend to get a bit of a
sell-off once credit default once the
credit default swap index crosses above
the 50-day moving average? Skipper, what
are your thoughts on this?" So, this
makes a ton of sense. Yeah, I don't know
I don't have any I don't have any
comments about the 50-day moving
average, but from the perspective of our
volatility just a momentum signal, which
we use to measure and map trends in
financial markets, you know, two of the
indicators four of the indicators in our
global macro risk matrix, which is the
tool we use to nowcast the market
regime, four of those indicators include
high yield and investment grade credit
spreads, which are very similar going to
be very correlated to credit default
swap rates as well as high yield and
investment grade yields, the actual
yields on those on those securities. And
so, you know, just you know, those are
two of the features that that filter
prominently in those models and when you
look at it from the
from the perspective of our positioning
model, investment grade credit spreads
are one of the top five of the top of
those 10 indicators that are featured in
the model in terms of the degree of
dispersion that they signal in major
bull market peaks and bear market
troughs, i.e., has one of the cleanest
reads
in terms of signaling to investors
whether or not you're at a at a bear
market peak or bull market sorry, bear
market trough or a bull market peak. So,
investors need to be aware that the
credit market is often a leading
indicator for big drawdowns in equity
markets. And so, obviously the signal
here that the the member is calling out
in terms of the the the upside breach of
the 50-day moving average in credit
default swaps, which again is a quite a
bit
>> [laughter]
>> quite a bit watered down relative to
what our VIX signal would indicate, but
that's neither here nor there. Uh You
know, that that's a big issue.
So, let me just wrap up and see if I can
find investment grade credit spread or
these are These are high yield credit
spreads. Those are broken out to neutral
and have been neutral for quite a while.
And then investment grade credit spreads
Oh, no, so that These are These are
investment grade spreads. So, these are
investment grade credit spreads
according to our VIX index. So, they've
been neutral really for a while now. And
so, ultimately if we can continue to see
a positive uplift here, positive trend
higher in this in this indicator, that's
going to be that's going to spell
trouble for broader financial markets.
And so, investors need to be aware
of these of these signals. You're not
really seeing much of a move in high
yield credit spreads yet. And that makes
a lot of sense in the context of the S&P
100 being near its all-time high. So,
you know, just be aware that we're no
longer in bearish VIX condition from the
set above investment grade credit
spreads or
high yield credit spreads. And so, the
fact that obviously that we've broken
out to neutral VIX is an issue in the
context of our nowcasting of the market
regime which very which in our opinion
is likely to transition to a risk-off
market regime at some point in the
coming months, but perhaps not before a
short squeeze to rinse out some of this
some of this hyper bearish positioning
that has built up on the short side. So,
we'll wrap it up there. Darius Dale here
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
July 21st, 2026. Best of luck out there
today. We'll catch back here tomorrow.
